"Give me your seed corn," Fox-Brother crooned, "and I shall plant it in the Sun-God’s secret garden, returning tenfold by moonrise." Bear, desperate and trusting, handed over the sacred sack. Fox-Brother vanished, feasting on the corn alone while the village wept.

The betrayal was perfect, but the Sun-God sees all shadows. Angered by the theft of his name, the deity cursed Fox-Brother: his belly would forever burn with hunger unless fed gold. Bear found the starving trickster and learned the divine sentence. To punish Fox-Brother, Bear had to melt his own golden armor—his only defense against winter wolves—and pour it down the trickster’s throat.

The punishment was terrible for both. Fox-Brother solidified into a statue of gilded agony, trapped in eternal heaviness. But Bear, now armorless, perished in the first snows. The cycle ended there, for the land learned a harsh truth: vengeance often costs the righteous as much as the wicked. The golden fox remains in the valley, a silent warning that the price of exploitation is mutual destruction.

It worked perfectly for one winter; the villagers shivered, grateful the Sun hadn’t abandoned them entirely, while Fox grew fat and warm.

But the Moon, who sees all things hidden in darkness, noticed the unnatural glow of Fox’s cloak at night. Offended by the theft of her brother’s light, she descended. She did not strike Fox down. The punishment was terrible. To strip the heat from Fox’s cloak, the villagers had to burn their harvest, their homes, and their history. The cost of justice was nearly as high as the crime. When Fox finally stood shivering and naked, the villagers did not kill him. They were too exhausted, their resources spent on the cure. They simply turned their backs, realizing that vengeance was too expensive to afford twice, and left him to the true cold.

But the cost was severe. Without the sheltering peaks, the bitter winds scoured the valley, making life nearly impossible. The villagers stood amidst the rubble of their protection, shivering but reclaiming their silence. The herd learned that trusting a liar costs lives, but correcting the lie costs everything else.
